description:
The Akira ransomware group is said to have emerged in March 2023, and there's much speculation about its ties to the former CONTI ransomware group.

It's worth noting that with the end of CONTI's operation, several affiliates migrated to independent campaigns such as Royal, BlackBasta, and others.

According to some reports, Akira affiliates also work with other ransomware operations, such as Snatch and BlackByte, as an open directory of tools used by an Akira operator was identified, which also had connections to the Snatch ransomware.

The first version of the Akira ransomware was written in C++ and appended files with the '.akira' extension, creating a ransom note named 'akira_readme.txt,' partially based on the Conti V2 source code. However, on June 29, 2023, a decryptor for this version was reportedly released by Avast.

Subsequently, a version was released that fixed the decryption flaw on July 2, 2023. Since then, the new version is said to be written in Rust, this time called 'megazord.exe,' and it changes the extension to '.powerranges' for encrypted files.

Most of Akira's initial access vectors use brute-force attempts on Cisco VPN devices (which use single-factor authentication only).
Additionally, exploitation of CVEs: CVE-2019-6693 and CVE-2022-40684 for initial access has been identified.
Source: https://github.com/crocodyli/ThreatActors-TTPs
name: akira

techniques.technique_details: Utilizes compromised VPN credentials.
Operators use obtained domain accounts for access.
Actors exploit CVE-2023-20269 remote service vulnerabilities.
Targets vulnerable CISCO devices via CVE-2023-20269.
Actors may use WMI to continue the attack.
Accepts parameters for its routines such as "-n 10" (for encryption percentage) or "-s (filename)" (for shared folder encryption).
Operators use PowerShell to launch commands to continue operations.
Akira ransomware uses service execution for persistence.
Operators use CMD to launch commands to continue operations.
techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell

techniques.technique_details: Upon initial access, Akira operators create a local account on the compromised system.
Upon initial access, Akira operators create a domain account on the compromised system.
Utilizes valid domain accounts for privilege escalation.
Utilizes local domain accounts for privilege escalation.
Uses commands in its operation to modify registries.
Usage of PowerTool or a KillAV tool abusing the Zemana AntiMalware driver to terminate AV-related processes was observed.
techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

techniques.technique_details: Uses Mimikatz, LaZagne, or a command line to dump LSASS from memory.
Uses Advanced IP Scanner and MASSCAN to discover remote systems.
Uses PCHunter and SharpHound to collect system information.
Uses AdFind, Windows net command, and nltest to collect domain information.
Utilizes remote services for accessing accounts and machines through remote services.
Uses RDP to move laterally within the victim's network.
Utilizes discovery to gather information for exfiltration.
techniques.technique_name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility

techniques.technique_details: Utilizes FileZilla or WinSCP to exfiltrate stolen information via FTP.
techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol

techniques.technique_details: Uses RClone to exfiltrate stolen information via a web service.
techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage

techniques.technique_details: Utilizes AnyDesk, Radmin, Cloudflare Tunnel, MobaXterm, RustDesk, or Ngrok to gain remote access on targeted systems.
Akira ransomware is used to encrypt files.
Deletes shadow copies to inhibit recovery.
